input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Infrastructure assets within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, exemplified by buildings, spanning the entire lifecycle from initial design through construction to operational phases; characterized by heterogeneous data sources and models requiring integration for real-time simulation, analysis, and optimization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Services include real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, simulation, analysis, and optimization for infrastructure management; targeting AECO stakeholders to enable data-driven decision-making, reduced operational costs, and enhanced collaboration across the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Scalable deployment from individual components to entire infrastructure systems with a centralized DT Framework coordinating roles including Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system employs a modular architecture with a centralized DT Framework acting as a hub for bidirectional data exchange, integrating components via standardized extension points. Runtime configuration management enables dynamic adjustments without redeployment, coordinating roles including Building Control Engineers for device modeling, Facility Managers for monitoring and control, and DT Engineers for service extension.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability across heterogeneous systems via standardized extension points for custom service integration, maintainability through low-overhead updates.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: BIM models are used to define sensors, actuators, and controllers, creating a unified semantic foundation. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles extract structured representations from enriched BIM models to generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Real-time data exchange ensures continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, enabling systematic embedding of live operational data from physical assets into the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Mechanisms feed control signals back to the Building Control System (BCS) through bidirectional communication channels between virtual and physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Eclipse Hono and Ditto are integrated to provide robust connectivity for real-time data exchange across heterogeneous systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: A model-driven deployment framework generates runtime interfaces for scalable digital twin deployment of building infrastructure systems.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle spans initial BIM-based device modeling (sensors, actuators, controllers), Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)-driven transformation to structured representations and deployable artifacts, and operational phases featuring real-time data synchronization and interactive visualization. Stakeholder roles (Building Control Engineers for modeling and control logic, Facility Managers for monitoring and operation, DT Engineers for custom services) define lifecycle governance responsibilities with transitions managed through automated MDE processes and modular architecture.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process utilizes Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to automatically generate deployable DT artifacts from enriched BIM models with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed via a modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, enabling adaptation to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real-time data exchange ensures continuous synchronization between physical and digital assets, supported by interactive visualization for monitoring and control. Custom analytics services integrated through standardized extension points provide decision support, though specific insight types, processing methodologies, or confidence measures are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework incorporates standardized extension points for interoperable custom service integration, but no specific industry standards (e.g., ISO, IFC), compliance frameworks, or certification requirements are referenced in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the AECO domain and infrastructure assets as the system under study, referencing the lifecycle from design to operations and the need for integrating heterogeneous data sources for real-time simulation and optimization. This is well-supported by the abstract and introduction which state DTs are central to the AECO domain, enabling 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization of building operations' and address 'fragmented data sources'.

The extracted services align with the text, which mentions 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization' and 'predictive analytics'. The description of targeting AECO stakeholders for data-driven decision-making and reduced costs is also supported by the text's mention of 'data-driven infrastructure management' and 'reduced operational costs'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly names several key tools and technologies used in the framework, including Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine, and BIM. Quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted description accurately reflects the text's description of temporal synchronization using specific terminology like 'real-time' and 'continuous synchronization'. While it does not provide specific latency numbers or frequencies (as none are in the text), it correctly captures the temporal behavior described.

The description correctly identifies the scope of scalability and the roles involved. The source text mentions 'scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems' and explicitly lists the coordinating roles in a table: 'Building Control Engineer', 'Facility Manager', and 'DT Engineer'.

The extracted description provides a comprehensive and accurate summary of the system's orchestration, combining the centralized hub architecture, modular design, extension points, and runtime configuration. It correctly integrates the specific roles and responsibilities defined in the source text.

The description aligns with the source's discussion on interoperability and maintainability. The text notes 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' and 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'. It also lists 'maintainability through low-overhead updates' as a non-functional requirement.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately summarizes the system's model-driven approach, detailing the use of BIM for semantic definition, MDE for artifact generation, and real-time data exchange. It is highly specific and strongly grounded in the provided sources. Supported by quotes: 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models', 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The source documents mention the capability to model and define actuators within the BIM environment, but they do not describe the physical acting components themselves, their specific types, control capabilities, actuation ranges, limitations, or safety constraints as required by the characteristic definition. The text focuses on the modeling framework rather than the physical hardware specifications. Relevant quote: 'This enables building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'.

The documents refer to sensors and sensor configurations in the context of BIM modeling and data exchange, but do not provide details on specific sensor types, measurement capabilities, spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, or accuracy specifications. The information present is too generic to fulfill the characteristic's requirement for describing the sensing infrastructure. Relevant quote: 'systematically embedded... runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations... into BIM models'.

The document mentions evaluating the 'entanglement' of the DT using a specific metric, but it does not address model fidelity requirements, uncertainty sources, error detection and correction mechanisms, or general quality assurance processes for the models. The ODTE metric measures connectivity/entanglement rather than fidelity or validity in the sense of accuracy and uncertainty. Relevant quote: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

While it mentions 'governance' in the context of MDE ensuring architectural compliance, it does not contain information regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection, or consent management. Therefore, the extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The source documents describe the technical framework components such as 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and metadata extraction like 'tenant and device ID'.
